column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Bluestone Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from https://www.z-beam.com/materials/stone/sedimentary/bluestone-laser-cleaning

Optimal average power for Bluestone cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Bluestone mineral absorption
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage
Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal
Nanosecond pulse width for controlled ablation of contaminants

Recommended number of passes for complete cleaning
Optimal beam overlap for uniform coverage

Typical range for industrial cleaning of natural stones like Bluestone using 1064 nm fiber lasers; starting at 100W balances efficiency and safety to avoid thermal damage to porous substrate. Max limited by Bluestone's low thermal conductivity (~1-2 W/m·K) to prevent cracking. Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics YLR series and academic review in 'Laser Cleaning of Stone Surfaces' (J. Cult. Herit., 2018).

Effective speeds for contamination removal on stone; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy rust/graffiti, faster (2000-3000 mm/s) for light dust. Recommended 1500 mm/s for optimal balance on Bluestone to ensure uniform cleaning without overheating. Source: Industry standards from Adapt Laser Systems manuals and case studies in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2020).

Nanosecond regime (10-200 ns) preferred for cost-effective cleaning of stones; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces heat-affected zone (HAZ) but increases cost. 50 ns recommended for Bluestone to minimize thermal diffusion given its melting point ~1200-1400°C. Source: Laser physics research in Appl. Phys. A (2019) on stone ablation thresholds.

Typical for pulsed cleaning to control energy delivery; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up process but risk heat accumulation on low-conductivity Bluestone. 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Trade-off: high freq increases throughput but requires monitoring for substrate heating. Source: Academic literature in J. Appl. Phys. (2021) on pulse repetition in heritage cleaning.

Fluence range for safe removal of contaminants like soot or oxides; Bluestone damage threshold ~4-5 J/cm² due to moderate reflectivity (~20-30% at 1064 nm). Recommended 1.5 J/cm² avoids ablation of substrate while effective for industrial prep. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. (2017) for sandstones.

Beam diameter range for cleaning; smaller (100-200 μm) for fine details on textured Bluestone, larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm recommended for uniform coverage in surface preparation. Source: Equipment specifications from CleanLaser CL series and process optimization in Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. (2022).

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers to build fluence gradually and prevent damage. 2 passes recommended for Bluestone to ensure complete removal without excessive heat exposure. Source: Industry practice in case studies from Laser Cleaning Europe consortium and Opt. Express (2019).

Overlap ensures uniform fluence distribution; 30-50% for fast large-area cleaning, 60-80% for detailed work on irregular Bluestone surfaces. 60% recommended for even coverage in contamination removal. Source: Process optimization studies in J. Laser Appl. (2020) for stone substrates.
